reason: The paper does use impurity/Gini importance explicitly ('mean decrease in node impurity, using the Gini index as the splitting rule') and does lean on rank positions (NND '7th most important', interspecific NNDs '14th, 16th and 26th') to support the primary/secondary/tertiary factor conclusions. However, the central qualitative conclusion, that vegetation/topographic (first-order) factors dominate over intraspecific (second-order) and especially interspecific (third-order) factors, is independently corroborated by a completely separate, non-impurity-based method: the binomial GLM/AICc forward-selection approach. That fully independent model, immune to Gini/impurity bias, converged on the same story: out of 118 candidate variables only two survived as significant predictors, PC1 ('complexity-maturity of forest', a first-order vegetation/structure factor) and total Eucalyptus density at the nest stand, both first-order factors, while intraspecific and interspecific NND variables did not enter the parsimonious model at all, consistent with the RF finding that interspecific NNDs rank low (14th/16th/26th). This cross-method agreement, together with the PDPs illustrating plausible marginal effects for the top factors, constitutes adequate corroboration of the RF ranking's substantive conclusion, even though the exact fine-grained numeric ranks are not separately validated. Because the ranking is adequately corroborated, criterion (3) fails, so the flag is refuted.
